title: Chapter Thirteen: Return to Maren
content: I chose it.
That's the part that matters. That's the part that changed everything.

Before that night in the attic, the retrocognition had always been something that *happened* to me — a current that seized my ankles and dragged me under before I could draw breath. I'd been drowning in Maren's memories since the day I touched the journal, pulled under by magic I didn't ask for and couldn't control. Victim of a gift I never wanted.
But I wasn't drowning anymore.

I sat cross-legged on the attic floor with Maren's journal open in my lap and the September wind shouldering through the cracked window. The leather was cool against my thighs, the pages furred with age, and I could smell the old ink — iron gall and oak, the kind that lasts centuries because it was made to cut into paper like a scar. The attic smelled of dust and dried lavender and something underneath both, something green and living that I'd learned to recognize as Maren herself, lingering in the fibers of everything she'd touched.
I placed my palm flat against the open page.
And I *pushed*.

Not with my body. With something deeper — something behind my sternum, something that beat in time with a pulse that wasn't mine. I reached for the thread I'd felt before, the one that connected my chest to 1762, and I pulled hand over hand until the attic dissolved like salt in water.

The fall was different this time. Controlled. I wasn't tumbling — I was descending. The attic didn't rip away; it softened. The floor became soil. The walls became hedges. The ceiling split into a sky so blue it hurt, and I landed on my knees in Maren Buena's garden with my hands pressed into the earth and my eyes full of 1762.

I'd seen this garden before. The flash in Chapter One — the herb beds and the stone wall and the woman sitting among the sage. But that had been a glimpse, a half-second of someone else's life snatched away from me before I could hold on. This was different. I was *here*. I could stay. I could act.
And there she was.
Maren Buena. The first. The wound that started all the wounds.

She sat with her back against the garden wall, knees drawn up, hands resting open on the soil as if she'd been waiting for something to fall into them. The herb beds surrounded her like a hospital ward — rosemary and sage and thyme, all the old healers, and she the patient they couldn't cure. She was young. Younger than me, maybe twenty, her dark hair coming loose from its braid, her hands calloused from work. Her eyes were dry — not because she wasn't sad, but because she'd cried all the tears a body could make and the wells had gone salt-empty.
And the ivy.
God, the ivy.

It wound from the base of the stone wall, up her neck, across her jaw, and sealed her mouth shut. Not tightly — not a gag or a punishment. It grew there. It *lived* there, roots threading into the mortar behind her head, vines curling soft and deliberate around her lips like a second skin. And it breathed. I could see it — the micro-contractions, the slow pulse of something alive against the tender skin above her chin. Each inhale she took, the ivy shifted. Each exhale, it settled. It had been doing this for so long that she'd stopped noticing. She breathed and it breathed with her, in and out, a suffocation so gentle she'd learned to call it quiet.

The garden smelled of sage and soil and silence. Not the absence of sound — the presence of it. Silence as a thing with weight and texture, pressing down on the herb beds, filling the spaces between the stalks. I could taste it on my tongue, green and mineral. The only sounds were the ivy breathing and, from inside the house, the distant rhythm of a man moving through rooms — heavy boots, a door closing, the clink of a bottle. Her husband. Home. Not coming outside.
She didn't see me at first. She was looking at the sage.

I walked to her. My feet made sound in the soil — real sound, real pressure — and her eyes tracked toward me without surprise. Two hundred and sixty years of silence, and someone appearing in her garden wasn't the strangest thing that had happened. Or maybe she'd been waiting. Maybe every woman who can't speak learns to watch the edges of the world, hoping something will come through.

I knelt beside her. The soil was cool and damp through my jeans. The sage brushed my elbow, releasing its sharp gray scent, and I could feel the herbs around us like witnesses — rosemary for remembrance, thyme for courage, sage for wisdom, all of them leaning in.
"You have something to say," I told her.
The ivy tightened.

It was subtle — a contraction, a pulling-close, the way a hand tightens around something fragile when someone reaches for it. The vines pressed deeper into the skin around her mouth, and I watched Maren's eyes close and open again, steady. She'd felt that before. Thousands of times. The word she couldn't say, the thought she couldn't voice, and each time the ivy reminded her: *No. Not you. Not allowed.*
But I wasn't asking her to speak.
"Then write it," I said.

I reached behind me, to the garden wall, where a piece of charcoal sat in the mortar gap — left there from the last time someone had marked the stones. From the last time someone had written on the world instead of speaking into it. I held it out to her.
Maren stared at the charcoal stick.

Her eyes were the driest eyes I've ever seen — not hard, not angry, just *empty* of tears, the way a riverbed goes empty after years of drought. But something moved behind them. Her fingers twitched against the soil. A recognition. Not of the charcoal itself — charcoal was ordinary, workaday, a thing you used to mark a cut on timber or tally a day's wages. But the *offer*. For two hundred and sixty years, no one had offered her a way to express herself that wasn't speech. Speech was the locked door. Speech was the ivy. And no one had thought to hand her another key.
Her hand rose. She took the charcoal.

It made a dry, soft sound in her grip — the whisper of carbon against skin. She turned it in her fingers, feeling its weight, its roughness, the way it left a faint black smear across her palm. Then she leaned forward and pressed it to the garden wall.
The charcoal met stone with a grit I felt in my teeth.
She wrote: **HE TOLD ME MY VOICE WAS UGLY.**

Five words. The letters were uneven — she hadn't written in so long, and the charcoal was blunt, and the stone resisted. But the words were there. I watched them appear, stroke by stroke, and with each stroke, the ivy around her mouth shifted. Not fell. Shifted. A loosening, the way a knot gives when you pick at it — not undone, but starting to recognize that it *could* be.
The scratch of charcoal on stone filled the garden. It was rough and intimate, the sound of a secret being told to a wall.
She wrote: **I WAS AFRAID.**

The ivy loosened further. I could see skin now — a sliver of her lower lip, pale and raw where the vines had held it. Maren's breath came faster, and the vines trembled with each exhale, losing their grip. She pressed the charcoal to the stones between the herb beds.
She wrote: **I STILL HAVE SOMETHING TO SAY.**

The words went on the wall. They went on the flat stones of the path. They went on the soil itself, carbon sinking into earth, and I watched her write and write and the ivy crack — not all at once, not like glass, but piece by piece, like ice on a spring river. First a vine fell from the corner of her mouth. Then another from her jaw. Then a coil slipped from her chin and landed in the sage, curling once before it went still. Each word was a thaw. Each letter was the river rising, and the ice couldn't hold.
She pulled the charcoal across the garden wall one more time, and she wrote the last word so hard the stick snapped in her fingers:
**LOUDER.**
The last vine fell.

It dropped from her lips like a rope cut from a mast, landing in her lap, and she breathed — mouth open, air rushing in — and the first breath she took in two hundred and sixty years made a sound like wind through a broken window, raw and real. She looked at me. Then she opened her mouth, and she spoke.
"**Louder.**"

One word. Her voice was cracked and raw and half-formed, scraped from a throat that hadn't shaped sound since before the American Revolution, and it was the most beautiful thing I have ever heard. It was rough and broken and young — god, she was so young — and it hit the garden like a stone hitting water, and the ripples went out through the sage and the rosemary and the thyme, and the herbs shivered, and the silence that had been a thing with weight *cracked* and fell away like the ivy, and there was sound in the garden again. Real sound. Her sound.

Maren's hand came to her throat, fingers pressing against the place where her voice lived, and she felt it vibrate, and she made another sound — not a word, just an *ah* of disbelief, of wonder — and tears came. Not the old tears. New ones. Fresh salt in dry wells, and they cut lines through the garden soil on her cheeks, and she was laughing and crying at once, and the sound was broken and whole and free.
I was crying too. I could taste it — salt and sage and charcoal dust, freedom on my tongue, bitter and bright.

She looked at her hands, still holding the broken charcoal. Then she looked at me. And the ivy on the ground — every vine, every leaf, every root that had sealed her mouth — turned gray and crumbled to powder. Not dead. *Released*. The ivy hadn't been her enemy. It had been a cage, and cages open when the prisoner walks free.
Maren Buena stood up.
She stood in her garden with the blue sky above her and the herbs around her and her voice in her mouth, and she wasn't a ghost. She wasn't a wound. She wasn't the first chapter of a curse.
She was a woman who could speak.

"Thank you," she said. Her voice was still cracked, still learning its own shape, but the words were clear. Each one a small miracle. "I heard you. Every time you read my journal. Every time you said my name. I heard you, and I couldn't answer."
"You're answering now," I said.
She smiled. It was a fierce, broken, beautiful thing. "Yes."
And then something changed.

Not in the garden — in *her*. The air around Maren brightened. Not with light, exactly, but with *presence*. She'd always been there, in the journal, in the house, but like a stain — a mark left behind. Now she was *present*. Deliberate. A stone set in the foundation, not a crack running through it. I could feel her — not as a memory, not as a flash of someone else's life, but as a force. A weight in the world that chose to be there.
"This is my gift," she said, and her voice was steadier now. "Not Voice-Binding. You've already unlocked that. This is the other part. The part the ivy was growing over."

She reached out and took my hand. Her palm was warm — warm in a way a ghost's shouldn't be, warm the way a living woman's hand should be. I could feel the calluses and the charcoal dust and something else, something that hummed like a tuning fork against my skin.
"For the seventh," Maren said. "When you need me. I'll be there."
Her grip tightened. "You won't go back alone. Not to any of them. I'll be in the pages. I'll be in the walls. When you reach for the next one — for any of them — I'll reach with you."
She let go of my hand. "But you have to choose it. Every time. You have to *choose* to go back. No one can pull you. No one can drown you. You are not a victim of this gift. You are its wielder."
I felt the pull then — the thread behind my sternum, warming, telling me the present was calling me home. The garden was starting to soften around the edges, the blue sky blurring like ink in water.
"Maren—"
"Go," she said. "Heal the others. I'll be in the book."

The garden folded. The sage and the soil and the stone and the sky compressed into a single point of green and gold, and I fell upward through two centuries and landed in the attic with my spine against the wall and the journal in my lap.
The first thing I noticed was the warmth.

The journal had always been cool — old leather, old paper, the temperature of things kept in attics and forgotten. But now it was warm against my thighs, warm against my palms, warm the way something alive should be warm. I could feel it breathing. Not metaphorically. The leather rose and fell in a slow, steady rhythm, and when I pressed my thumb against the spine, I felt a pulse.
I opened it.
The pages were the same — Maren's handwriting, her herbal remedies, her quiet records of a life lived in silence. But the last page was different. The last page had new words.
Not old ink. Not iron gall. This was fresh — dark and warm, written in a hand that hadn't touched paper in two hundred and sixty years.
**The first wound is silence. The first healing is voice. Use mine.**
I read it three times. Then I closed the journal and held it against my chest, my arms wrapped around the leather, my chin resting on the spine. And I felt it — steady, slow, undeniable.
Maren's heartbeat.

Under the leather. Under the pages. Under two hundred and sixty years of silence, a heart was beating, and I held it against mine, and they matched — hers and mine, ancestor and daughter, wound and healing, silence and sound.
The first wound is silence. The first healing is voice.
One down. Six to go.

And I knew — not hoped, not wondered, *knew* — that the curse was not irreversible. The ivy could fall. The charcoal could write. The voice could return. I'd seen it. I'd felt it. I'd held the heartbeat in my hands.
For the first time since the magic had found me, I wasn't afraid of what I could do.
I was ready to do more.
